 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-9syku.11.3.1","title":"[FCP3.MIGRATE] Request-response connector family migration wave","description":"## Goal
Migrate the request-response connector families onto the FCP3 SDK and ABI after the request-response pilot proves the model.

## Why this exists
This should be the first broad migration wave because it offers the fastest deletion wins and the clearest chance to validate the common-case authoring story before more complex connector families move.

## Scope
- group low-state, RPC-style connectors into ordered migration batches
- prefer families that let us delete the most compatibility code fastest
- require unit, host-backed integration, and replayable e2e evidence with detailed logs for each migrated family
- record intentional behavior changes instead of hiding them as accidental drift

## Acceptance criteria
A completed batch leaves less compatibility code behind, has replayable evidence for success and failure paths, and improves the default connector authoring story for the rest of the migration program.","notes":"## FCP3 execution note
This bead is part of the FCP3 re-foundation and must remain self-contained. Future implementers should not need to consult the original planning conversation to understand intent, scope, architectural posture, or why the work matters.

## No-compat rule
Do not preserve backwards compatibility, dual-stack behavior, fallback paths, or migration-era abstractions beyond the minimum temporary quarantine explicitly called out elsewhere in the graph. Once a replacement path is proven, the old path should be deleted.

## Verification and evidence mandate
- Planning/spec/design beads must explicitly define the downstream verification story rather than hand-wave it.
- Implementation beads are not complete without focused unit/property tests, negative/failure-path coverage, and where relevant failure-injection coverage for cancellation, drain, restart, timeout, backpressure, failover, and recovery behavior.
- Host- or runtime-crossing changes must include end-to-end scripts with detailed structured logging, timestamps, digests/ids, receipts or health outcomes, and clear replay instructions.

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-9syku.11.3.2","title":"[FCP3.MIGRATE] Streaming connector family migration wave","description":"## Goal
Migrate the streaming connector families onto the FCP3 SDK and ABI after the streaming pilot proves the model.

## Why this exists
Streaming connectors are where transport semantics, backpressure, cancellation, reconnect, and operator visibility are easiest to get subtly wrong. This wave should apply the pilot lessons systematically across the fleet.

## Scope
- prioritize connectors with WebSocket, SSE, or long-lived bidirectional semantics
- require proof of backpressure, cancel, reconnect, drain, and recovery behavior under the new model
- require unit, integration, and replayable e2e evidence with detailed logs for each migrated family
- delete prior transport glue once each family is migrated and verified

## Acceptance criteria
Each migrated streaming family demonstrates the FCP3 transport and lifecycle model in production-shaped scenarios rather than only passing narrow happy-path tests.","notes":"## FCP3 execution note

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-9syku.11.3.3","title":"[FCP3.MIGRATE] Polling, webhook, and other stateful connector family migration wave","description":"## Goal
Migrate the polling, webhook, and other stateful or background connector families onto the FCP3 SDK and ABI after the stateful pilot proves the model.

## Why this exists
These connectors combine durability, scheduling, replay risk, and background lifecycle complexity. They should migrate only once the stateful patterns are well-proven and the evidence model is strong enough to diagnose subtle failures.

## Scope
- cursor-based polling, webhook receivers, and background-service-heavy connectors
- explicit proof of durable state, failover, drain, retry, idempotency, and receipt behavior
- unit, integration, and replayable e2e evidence with detailed logs for each migrated family
- deletion of old lifecycle or runtime glue after each family is migrated

## Acceptance criteria
A completed stateful migration batch leaves behind a connector family that is safer, more observable, and easier to operate than its pre-FCP3 version, with no fallback to old lifecycle glue.","notes":"## FCP3 execution note

Ensure README, AGENTS guidance, crate docs, runbooks, troubleshooting guides, and migration notes all reflect FCP3-only reality once the redesign direction is locked.

## Why this exists
A clean implementation still fails users if the docs teach the wrong mental model. This bead exists to make the public and internal guidance match the real system, including how to provision connectors, interpret denials, run diagnostics, and replay failures.

## Scope
- README architecture and onboarding descriptions
- AGENTS.md project guidance where FCP3 changes repo workflow or architectural truth
- crate and module docs that still describe compatibility-first behavior
- runbooks, troubleshooting material, and operator-oriented reference docs for explain, drain, repair, and replay flows
- elimination of stale wording that implies old execution models remain blessed

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-9syku.13","title":"[FCP3] Portable connector execution and mesh placement across local, WASI, and remote nodes","description":"# [FCP3] Portable Connector Execution and Mesh Placement

## Goal
Define how connector apps are placed, moved, resumed, failed over, and fenced across local, WASI, and remote mesh execution environments under the FCP3 model.

## Why this matters
FCP is not only a local host/runtime redesign. The platform vision includes zone-aware execution across a trusted mesh. This deserves a first-class workstream rather than being buried as a footnote under policy or transport mapping.

## Scope
- placement planner and device/zone fitness model
- local versus remote execution selection rules
- leases, checkpoints, and failover semantics for connector apps with state or singleton constraints
- interaction with host supervision, manifest constraints, and transport/addressing","notes":"## FCP3 execution note

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-9syku.13.1","title":"[FCP3.PLACEMENT] Planner and device/zone fitness model for connector app placement","description":"## Goal
Define the placement planner model for selecting execution environment and host node for a connector app.

## Why this exists
Placement is not just a scheduling problem. In FCP3 it is where security, resource fit, transport capabilities, locality, durability constraints, and operator expectations all meet.

## Scope
- device capability and resource fitness across local, WASI, embedded, and remote execution candidates
- zone affinity, isolation, and policy constraints derived from runtime authority and sandbox rules
- manifest-driven execution requirements, transport fitness, and operational safety constraints
- locality, latency, tenancy, and durability tradeoffs for different connector classes
- explainability for placement decisions and denials in language usable by operators and agents

## Deliverables
- planner objective and constraint model
- representative placement scenarios and corner cases, including denial and degraded-fit outcomes
- deterministic test expectations and evidence surfaces for explaining planner decisions

## Acceptance criteria
A future implementer can build the placement planner so it is predictable, explainable, security-aligned, and testable rather than an opaque scoring heuristic.","notes":"## FCP3 execution note

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-9syku.13.2","title":"[FCP3.PLACEMENT] Failover, lease, checkpoint, and migration semantics for portable connector apps","description":"## Goal
Define the failover, lease, checkpoint, and migration semantics for portable connector apps.

## Why this exists
Portable execution becomes dangerous as soon as connectors have durable state, single-writer requirements, or background work. This bead makes those risks explicit so portability does not quietly introduce split-brain or data-loss behavior.

## Scope
- when stateful connector apps may move and when movement must be forbidden
- how singleton-writer or fenced connectors fail over without split-brain
- what checkpoints, drains, receipts, and lease transitions are required before or after movement
- what operator-visible evidence must exist for failover, migration, denial, or rejected movement
- what must be rejected outright because it would weaken the security or correctness model

## Acceptance criteria
A future implementer can build failover and migration logic that is safe, diagnosable, and explicitly constrained rather than implicitly optimistic.","notes":"## FCP3 execution note
This bead is part of the FCP3 re-foundation and must remain self-contained. Future implementers should not need to consult the original planning conversation to understand intent, scope, architectural posture, or why the work matters.

## No-compat rule
Do not preserve backwards compatibility, dual-stack behavior, fallback paths, or migration-era abstractions beyond the minimum temporary quarantine explicitly called out elsewhere in the graph. Once a replacement path is proven, the old path should be deleted.

## Verification and evidence mandate
- Planning/spec/design beads must explicitly define the downstream verification story rather than hand-wave it.
- Implementation beads are not complete without focused unit/property tests, negative/failure-path coverage, and where relevant failure-injection coverage for cancellation, drain, restart, timeout, backpressure, failover, and recovery behavior.
- Host- or runtime-crossing changes must include end-to-end scripts with detailed structured logging, timestamps, digests/ids, receipts or health outcomes, and clear replay instructions.
- Evidence must be redaction-safe, easy to inspect, and sufficient for a future engineer to diagnose failures without re-deriving the intended behavior.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:56:36.811267Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-03-12T23:14:35.755598Z","closed_at":"2026-03-12T23:14:35.755293Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["failover","fcp3","leases","placement"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-9syku.13.2","depends_on_id":"flywheel_connectors-9syku.13","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:56:36.811267Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-9syku.13.2","depends_on_id":"flywheel_connectors-9syku.13.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:56:49.589766Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-9syku.13.2","depends_on_id":"flywheel_connectors-9syku.3.4","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-09T21:26:14.134640Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-9syku.13.2","depends_on_id":"flywheel_connectors-9syku.3.5","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-09T21:26:13.680289Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-9syku.13.2","depends_on_id":"flywheel_connectors-9syku.4.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:56:49.726324Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":1470,"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-9syku.13.2","author":"RainyGate","text":"[Alien-graveyard uplift]

Best-fit distributed-systems additions from the graveyard are **§13.7 SWIM**, **§13.9 Anti-Entropy Replication**, **§8.11 Immutable Log-Structured DB**, and where quotas/leases need hard bounds, **§8.12 Escrow / Bounded-Counter CRDTs**.

Concrete implications for this bead:
- use SWIM-style membership/suspicion as the lightweight failure-detector input to placement/failover, not ad hoc liveness guesses
- use Merkle/anti-entropy reconciliation for checkpoint/state divergence detection before permitting resume or migration
- treat portable state/checkpoints as append-only, structurally shared artifacts where possible so migration and evidence remain auditable
- for lease pools / bounded movable capacity, prefer escrow-style token reservation semantics over optimistic counters
- fallback-safe mode should forbid movement whenever membership is unstable, reconciliation is incomplete, or escrow/lease invariants cannot be proven

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-bngd","title":"TEST: Discord Connector Integration Tests","description":"# TEST: Discord Connector Integration Tests (Deterministic)


## Goal
Validate `fcp.discord` integration behavior (gateway + REST) under deterministic mocks, including event tainting and approval-gated writes.

## External surface (NetworkConstraints)
Default deny.
- Network egress MUST be restricted per-operation via manifest `NetworkConstraints`.
- Allow only the configured service host(s); HTTPS-only where applicable; deny redirects by default.
- Bound timeouts + response sizes; treat all remote responses as untrusted input; never leak secrets/PII in logs or errors.

## Zone model (mechanical)

<!-- fcp2-single-zone-note -->
**FCP2 single-zone binding note:** A connector instance binds to exactly one `ZoneId` for its entire lifetime.
The connector MUST NOT multiplex multiple zones internally.

Recommended deployment pattern: run separate connector instances in separate zones with different capability grants/policies.

## Purpose
Integration tests for `fcp.discord` using:
- a deterministic mock Discord REST API (wiremock or in-process)
- a deterministic mock Discord Gateway (WebSocket server)

No real Discord calls are allowed.

## What this suite must prove
### 1) Gateway correctness
- IDENTIFY / RESUME handling
- heartbeat timing + ack handling
- sequence number tracking and persistence
- reconnect/backoff behavior is bounded

### 2) REST correctness
- request formation for send_message / reactions / channel list
- error mapping (401/403/429/5xx → FCP error taxonomy)
- Retry-After compliance

### 3) Singleton-writer state
- only the lease-holder maintains the gateway connection
- restart resumes safely (or fails closed if resume is unsafe)

### 4) FCP2 connector contract
- standard method surface end-to-end
- capability gating (deny without token, allow with token)
- events emitted as EventEnvelopes with correct topics
- inbound events tainted as external input

## Required adversity cases
- global rate limit
- bucketed rate limit
- malformed gateway frames
- mid-stream disconnect during event emission

## Logging requirements
- Structured JSON logs with correlation IDs.
- Never log bot tokens or raw message bodies by default.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Deterministic in CI.
- Failures are actionable (DecisionReceipt reason_code + evidence surfaced).

+{"id":"flywheel_connectors-bs5ih","title":"[FCP3-V3] Universal Harmonization: Complete runtime, error, and transport migration","description":"## Goal

+{"id":"flywheel_connectors-bs5ih.1","title":"[FCP3-V3.1] ConnectorRuntime lifecycle adoption across all 89 connectors","description":"## Goal

Every connector must create a ConnectorRuntime during configure() and use it for request contexts, background contexts, and graceful shutdown. Currently only anthropic and openai have this.

## Why this matters

ConnectorRuntime provides:
- Deadline-aware request contexts (prevents unbounded waits)
- Background context for streaming/polling (cancellation-aware)
- Graceful shutdown propagation
- Structured tracing for all lifecycle events

Without it, connectors use ad-hoc timeout/cancellation patterns that are inconsistent and miss the FCP3 observability contract.

## Migration pattern (from crates/fcp-sdk/src/migration.rs)

In connector struct, add: `runtime: Option<ConnectorRuntime>`

In configure():
```rust
self.runtime = Some(ConnectorRuntime::new(ConnectorRuntimeConfig::default()));
```

In invoke/request paths:
```rust
let ctx = self.runtime.as_ref().unwrap().request_context();
```

In shutdown():
```rust
if let Some(rt) = &self.runtime { rt.shutdown(); }
```

## Acceptance criteria
- All 89 connectors have ConnectorRuntime in their struct
- All request paths use runtime.request_context()
- All shutdown paths call runtime.shutdown()
- All existing tests still pass","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-03-14T21:36:03.771743Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-03-14T21:36:03.771743Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["connectors","fcp3","runtime","v3"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-bs5ih.1","depends_on_id":"flywheel_connectors-bs5ih","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-14T21:36:03.771743Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-bvs","title":"Study: Clawdbot Connector Patterns - Synthesis & Recommendations","description":"# Study: Clawdbot Connector Patterns - Synthesis & Recommendations


## Goal
Study and synthesize Clawdbot connector patterns into actionable guidance for FCP2 connector design (security, state, tests).

Synthesize findings from Discord and Telegram connector studies into actionable patterns and recommendations for FCP connector development.

- Event dispatcher
- Configuration loader

#### Message Processing

- Response builder

#### Implementation Guidelines

- Testing requirements

#### Documentation Standards

5. **Migration Guide** - How to port existing connectors to FCP

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-c0fv","title":"[POL] Diff + risk summary engine","description":"# [POL] Diff + risk summary engine

## Goal
Provide deterministic diffs and risk summaries between policy bundles.

## Deliverables
- Diff engine (stable ordering) for:
  - Zone ceilings, capability grants, roles, and resource bindings.
  - Network constraints expansions (hosts/ports/CIDR changes).
  - Approval/elevation/declassification rules.
- Risk summary with reason codes and severity tiers.

## Design Notes
- Output must be stable and canonical for reproducible reviews.
- Risk summary flags widening egress, new risky caps, and lowered integrity.

## Tests
- Unit tests for diff correctness and ordering stability.
- Golden snapshot tests for representative bundle pairs.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Same inputs always yield identical diff output.
- Summary flags risky changes with clear reason codes.","notes":"[AlienArtifactPractical]
- Add evidence ledger fields with per-check log-odds contributions and explicit loss decision.
- Add anytime-valid risk control (e-process) for approvals/denials and record epsilon in receipts.
[ExtremeOptimizationPractical]

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-lszk.5","title":"STANDARD: Connector Spec Template (FCP2)","description":"# STANDARD: Connector Spec Template (FCP2)


## Goal
Provide the canonical copy/paste Beads template for planning V2-only connectors (single-zone, default deny, constraints, receipts/audit, tests/E2E).

## Purpose
A copy/paste template for planning **new FCP2 connectors** in Beads.

Goal: every connector plan should be mechanically consistent with FCP2 (single-zone binding, default deny, NetworkConstraints, receipts/audit, taint/approval), and should include tests/E2E from day one.

This template is intentionally independent of any specific external service.

---

## Required Bead Structure (recommended)
For each connector **epic**:
1. `<connector>.1` — **Manifest + Capability Map (FCP2)**
2. `<connector>.2` — **Provisioning Automation** (OAuth/webhooks/setup)
3. `<connector>.3` — **TEST: Unit/Integration Tests (mock-only)**
4. `<connector>.4` — **E2E: Connector Compliance Run**

For each connector **feature** bead under the epic (operations / slices):
- Add dependency: feature → `<connector>.1` (manifest/caps)
- Add dependency on `flywheel_connectors-1n78.33` if the feature requires **persistent cursor/dedupe/streaming state**.

---

## Template: Connector Epic Description

### 1) Goal
- One sentence: what this connector enables.

### 2) Hard Requirements (FCP2)
- **Single-zone binding** (exactly one `ZoneId` per connector instance).
- **Default deny**: every operation requires explicit CapabilityTokens.
- **Sandbox + NetworkConstraints** enforced mechanically.
- **Receipts + audit** for all writes/dangerous ops.
- **No secrets on disk**; strict log redaction.
- **Prefer secretless connectors**: credentials injected at the network boundary via `CredentialObject`/`CredentialId` (egress proxy) so raw secret bytes never enter connector memory.

### 3) Manifest Archetypes (closed set)
List which apply (only from):
- `bidirectional`, `streaming`, `operational`, `storage`, `knowledge`

(“webhook/polling/request-response/database” are interaction patterns, not archetypes.)

### 4) External Surface (mechanical)
- NetworkConstraints allowlist (default deny): which hosts are permitted.
- Redirect policy (default deny to new hosts).
- Timeouts and max response sizes.

### 5) Capability Model
- Capability families and per-operation required caps.
- **Rule**: capability IDs MUST NOT encode hostnames/ports.

### 6) State Model
Declare one:
- `stateless`
- `singleton_writer` (requires lease)
- `crdt` (rare; justify)

List what state is persisted (dedupe keys, cursors, mapping tables) and how to bound it.

### 7) Security / Taint / Approval
- Identify tainted inputs (webhooks, external content, untrusted metadata).
- Define what requires ApprovalToken vs is safe.

### 8) Observability
- Structured JSON logs with correlation IDs.
- Reason codes for denials.

### 9) Testing Strategy (required)
- Unit/integration tests are **mock-only**.
- E2E compliance run uses the shared runner and produces evidence bundle.

### 10) Acceptance Criteria
- “Passes compliance runner (static + dynamic).”
- “No real external calls in unit tests.”
- “Dangerous ops audited/receipted and approval gated.”

---

## Template: Manifest + Capability Map Bead (`<connector>.1`)
- Full operation list + schema IDs
- Capability families
- NetworkConstraints per operation
- Sandbox profile requirements
- Good/bad manifest examples for static compliance

---

## Template: Provisioning Bead (`<connector>.2`)
- Auth setup (preferred): create `CredentialObject` + `CredentialId` and rely on MeshNode egress-proxy credential injection ("secretless" connectors)
- Webhook/OAuth automation
- `fcp doctor` readiness checks
- Fully mockable for unit tests

---

## Template: Test Bead (`<connector>.3`)
- Mock servers for external API
- Golden vectors for:
  - signature validation
  - redaction
  - idempotency
  - pagination
  - streaming bounds/backpressure

---

## Template: E2E Compliance Bead (`<connector>.4`)
- Run shared compliance runner
- Validate:
  - default deny
  - NetworkConstraints
  - receipts/audit
  - structured logs
- Capture artifacts bundle
